title: [CI:DOCS] Multi-arch: Always push updated version-tagged image
body:
#### What type of PR is this?

/kind other

#### What this PR does / why we need it:

Given a near daily release of Fedora updates, some addressing security
issues, it's safest for users to always have an updated version-tagged
image.  The cost of this is more image churn, but for a relatively small
image this should hopefully be a minor burden vs improved security.

Note: This is not a perfect solution, as there may be no
hard-dependencies between buildah and other packages with security
updates.  However it is an improvement over the previous regime where we
simply pushed the latest version-tagged image once, then never touched
it again.

Minor: Rename `gen_fqin` identifier to `reponame_reg` to be more
consistent with it's sister block `containers_reg`.

#### How to verify it

Merge the PR and if it fails, fix it in a new PR :sob: 
(this is github's fault)

#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:

Fixes #3365

#### Special notes for your reviewer:

* Github-actions workflow is very head-hurtie to review / understand.  I apologize in advance :disappointed: 
* Depends on #3385

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

None

/cc @rostrovsky

comments.body:
@cevich: GitHub didn't allow me to request PR reviews from the following users: rostrovsky.

Note that only [containers members](https://github.com/orgs/containers/people) and repo collaborators can review this PR, and authors cannot review their own PRs.

<details>

In response to [this](https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/3381):

>#### What type of PR is this?
>
>/kind other
>
>#### What this PR does / why we need it:
>
>These images contain a plethora of software components in addition and
>in support of the primary command (`buildah` in this case).  Due to
>this complexity, it's possible (likely even) that some other component
>will require an update due to (worst-case) a security fix.  In other
>words, there is no hard association maintained between the `buildah`
>version and all the other components of the image.
>
>Prior to this commit, new version-tagged container images were simply
>pushed and then never ever touched again.  This leads to a situation
>where unrelated or loosely related security fixes are not realized in
>the images for a potentially very long time (if ever).
>
>At the same time, if automation always pushed the latest/greatest
>version-tagged image, it would cause unnecessary pulls from clients even
>if only the timestamp changed.  Improve this situation by tracking
>a hash of the sorted package listing, within image metadata.  This value
>may then be used for comparison in automation, when determining
>whether or not to push a "new" version-tagged image.
>
>#### How to verify it
>
>Merge the PR and if it fails, fix it in a new PR :sob: 
>(this is github's fault)
>
>#### Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
>
>Fixes #3365
>
>#### Special notes for your reviewer:
>
>Github-actions workflow is very head-hurtie to review / understand.  I apologize in advance :disappointed: 
>
>#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
>
>None
>
>/cc @rostrovsky


Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

@rhatdan @TomSweeneyRedHat @edsantiago PTAL at this draft when you have a full cup of caffeinated liquid.  I honestly question the value of this change compared to just always pushing the latest + version-tagged image daily.  In other words, I think new packages come out frequently enough, the two schemes may be equivalent but the one in this PR is significantly more complex.

As an alternative (also addressing #3365), when overwriting an existing version-tagged image, we could instead do it with an image built from a very simple docker-file:

```
FROM <existing image FQIN>
RUN dnf upgrade -y && dnf clean all
```

The effect would be, users whom already have the old image cached, only need to pull the new layer.  The (big) down-side to this approach is, even more complexity in an already very difficult to review and maintain github action workflow.  Remember: I'm working on a Cirrus-CI based replacement for all this, so we could decide to do something like this idea, there instead.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fbuildah). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/buildah/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [cevich] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

After sitting and thinking about this for a day, I think the extra complexity here isn't woth it.  Fighting with github-actions isn't a long-term solution, it's just not an easily maintainable architecture.  I'm going to close this and replace it with a PR which simply updates the current version-tagged image and `latest` on a daily basis.

That way we incorporate all released security updates to the current release version, keeping our users safe at the cost of possibly waiting to refresh local copies of a ~200M image.  This also will be better aligned with the future direction I want to take (stated above): Moving this automation into Cirrus-cron jobs, where existing version-tagged images simply get a new 'update' layer added.  The logic to do that is a bit complex, so having it under Cirrus will be necessary given the greatly improved readability and debug-ability.

> I'm going to close this and replace it

Chose to just re-implement in place.

@edsantiago I hate to say your previous 'LGTM' may no-longer be valid.  I re-wrote this vs the original draft, so it will simply always push the version-tagged images daily.  I plan to deal with the churn more elegantly in the Cirrus-cron based implementation.
Wow...I searched and searched...there is literally no other way to set an env. var. value containing more than one line :sob:

@edsantiago is that now a little more sensible?

(Remember, I still intend to trash this completely with a simple bash implementation running under Cirrus-cron)
(small tweak...replaced one more stupid "context expression" with a simple built-in env. variable)
LGTM but I'll defer slash-lgtm pending resolution of @TomSweeneyRedHat's concerns
I'm not hip on the version overwriting, let's talk.  Perhaps with @rhatdan if we can.  The rest looks fine to me.

Just for the record, I'll whine one last time on this.  In the past, we had been updating the versioned images on a regular basis and had a number of customers say they could not use them until they became relatively static.  I do believe this change is a backstep and the wrong approach.
Ok, let's see if they complain.  Perhaps we drop it back to once a week or once a month.  But we don't want to have these images become ones that scanners report as full of CVEs.

@TomSweeneyRedHat I agree with you, but this is an ugly and unavoidable reality of the container-images world: once we push an image, we become responsible for everything inside it. Even though the buildah image (probably) doesn't use most of what's shipped in the base, if there's a privilege escalation zeroday in `/bin/true` we need to update our image.

With that said, I keep thinking there's a simple solution: strip our images to just the bare minimum (buildah + libraries). Can someone remind me why we don't do that? (Maybe this is a cabal topic).
Theoretically we could, but we are not really sure what people do within the container. For example most expect bash to be there.  Not sure what else.

> Theoretically we could, but we are not really sure what people do within the container. For example most expect bash to be there. Not sure what else.

This is exactly the thing.  The container image is **NOT** a pure buldah distribution mechanism (that what packages are for).  It's meant to be a small environment where you can actually do some minimal buildah-ing.  For that, we need the extra included bits...which means we're subjected to not placing users in known danger :confused: 

Also remember...this is not any kind of different/new problem that almost every other single container image in the universe faces.  You get 'latest', 'tags', and digest-based pulls.  You do ***NOT*** get both convenience/stability AND security at the same time.  So I think we can just let the whiners, whine.
